data.title: If I Had To Retire With 2 BDCs, These Would Be My Picks
data.description:
It seems that the risks for the BDC sector (BIZD) just keep emerging one after another. In the space of less than 2 years, the sector has been pressured by falling base rates, compressing spreads, stagnant M&A markets, enormous supply of new entrants, and, recently, increased credit risks from Tricolor and First Brands bankruptcies and weakening SaaS businesses, which play a significant role in BDC portfolios.

As a result of these dynamics, the BDC index has dropped by ~23% in the past 12-month period. This is a huge drawdown.

On top of this, 12 BDCs have delivered negative news on the dividend front, where even such names as Golub Capital (GBDC) and Gladstone Capital (GLAD) were forced to make these painful decisions. In the context of the publicly traded BDC space that consists of ~55 players, the fact that 12 BDCs have cut their dividends in the past 12-month period is significant.

Moreover, I firmly believe that there will be many more announcing these kinds of news as they report the Q4 and/or Q1, 2026 figures. The logic here is simple: 1) BDC sector average base dividend coverage is 100% (i.e., no margin of safety), 2) balance sheets are fully leveraged across the board, providing no ammunition to grow, and 3) lower base rates and continued spread compression will just keep pushing the NII per share generation down.

Now, the fact that BDCs are effectively leveraged vehicles that lend to companies that cannot access cheap bank or public capital market financing, in combination with the aforementioned backdrop, which shows how violent the moves in the BDC sector can be, provides a strong ground for excluding BDCs from retirement income portfolios.

In other words, the risks that come with BDC investments are theoretically too high for prudent retirement investors or investors, who cannot afford to play around with the portfolio capital, which is meant for living expense coverage.

Personally, as a structural BDC bull, I would say that this is a fair characterization. However, I completely disagree with the notion that all BDCs are unsuitable for truly reliable and durable current income streams.

In this article I will now try to articulate how select BDCs have what it takes to be considered "sleep well at night" retirement picks. The two BDCs that I will highlight below would most likely be the picks that I would choose if I was forced to invite only two BDCs into my high and durable passive income portfolio.

Pick #1: Capital Southwest, 11.2% yield

Capital Southwest (CSWC) is a BDC with one of the longest track records as a publicly traded vehicle in the entire BDC industry. It is, of course, nothing that moves the needle in the context of the overall investment case, but it at least allows us to take a look at how CSWC has performed in different market regimes.

This chart is really powerful. It shows that CSWC has outperformed the booming S&P 500 (SPY) index and that despite several disastrous events such as the dot-com bubble, GFC, and COVID-19, the BDC has managed to preserve NAV and regular (and enticing) dividend distributions.

The historical performance is not a guarantee of future results, but it certainly puts things into the right perspective when making an argument that CSWC is just a private credit vehicle that is set for a blow-out when the next credit crunch happens.

Now, if we speak about the underlying fundamentals, then this is where the essence lies. And the first thing with which we have to start is the internal management component, which is the main reason why I have chosen CSWC as a buy-and-forget retirement income pick over Fidus Investment (FDUS) - similar to FDUS, at a lower multiple, but externally managed.

The value of internal management is that there is simply better alignment of interest between management and shareholders, and, importantly, the fee structure is more favorable for long-term investors, where, say, in the case of abnormal returns, we can avoid value-restrictive (double-digit) incentive fees that are similar to hedge fund models.

For this reason, internally managed BDCs tend to trade at a premium to externally managed peers. Granted, it might not be the best setup for investors, who are looking for superior gains in the short to medium-term, but when the investment horizon is, say, 10+ years, the internal management factor should support alpha performance (or at least greater stability).

Furthermore, another driver for my preference towards CSWC is the fact that the BDC is equity-biased with a focus on lower middle market businesses, where the competition from the mushrooming private credit supply is inherently lower. For larger money managers, it really does not make sense to dedicate their resources to ~$5 million ticket-size funding, while their underlying portfolio is measured in billions. This dynamic introduces an important moat (or shield) around CSWC's business model, which, practically, manifests itself via higher and more stable spreads.

Now, speaking of the equity bias, I think that it is a huge benefit if a BDC has taken equity co-investments on top of standard loan injections. The equity component, by definition, comes with higher return potential, especially if the holding period is long enough. But more importantly, the equity exposure carries a negatively correlated return factor to traditional loan investments. For example, as the base rates go down, loan investments tend to yield less, thereby resulting in an NII yield compression. Conversely, for equities, lower yields result in higher valuations and a better environment to find traction in the market for successful monetization. Namely, equity investments, which for CSWC account for 9.1% of the total portfolio, can smooth out the return profile and neutralize the negative effects from non-accruals.

Finally, what I really value in CSWC is that it has consistently maintained a conservative leverage profile like now - at 1.07x (debt to equity), while the sector average is closer to 1.22x. This has two benefits: 1) lower sensitivity to potential downside risks, and 2) ability to go on offense when the time is right without entering speculative financial risk territory.

Pick #2: Trinity Capital, 13.2%

Trinity Capital Inc. (TRIN) is just like CSWC, an internally managed BDC, which is automatically a huge positive for long-term investors, who deploy capital for smooth and gradual compounding.

In fact, let me say that finding an internally managed BDC is not that easy or common, as there are only 4 such publicly traded vehicles available out there. I'm not including PhenixFIN (PFX) in the conversation, which is a different beast - i.e., different business model, tiny market cap, and, importantly, value-destructive historical profile (e.g., more than 60% down in the past 10-year period).

Now, the main difference between CSWC and TRIN lies in the investment strategy or scope, which is much wider and more diverse for TRIN:

TRIN has really built a diversified ecosystem of various lending and equity (warrant) co-investment opportunities, where equipment finance and asset based lending have recently gained a more pronounced momentum. Exactly these two areas have experienced significant growth in the past couple of years - mainly due to lower downside (better protection due to underlying collateral) and expanding CapEx within many asset-intensive businesses.

What the presence of multiple investment avenues brings to the table as well is the ability for TRIN to access a wider and larger origination universe from which to really select the best risk-adjusted investments.

We can see how this has borne fruit for TRIN in the context of portfolio and earnings quality. For example, as of Q3, 2025, TRIN had one of the lowest PIK shares in the entire sector - i.e., at 0.9% of the total income, while for the sector, the metric was 7.9%. Similarly, TRIN has only 2.5% of its portfolio (at cost) linked to non-accruals, while for the sector it is closer to 4%.

Another area, where TRIN's position of being able to cherry pick the most attractive investments has played out nicely is interest rate management.

Here comes an interesting chart:

Because of the favorable interest rate floors, TRIN is in a unique position to avoid negative pressures from lower base rates, while almost any other BDC in the sector is forced to deal with the consequences (i.e., NII per share going down due to lower SOFR).

In a nutshell, I would certainly include TRIN in my "2 BDC retirement income portfolio" alongside CSWC for durable and stress-free income returns as well as long-term compounding.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: DT Midstream: Another Sign Of An Old Bull Market
data.description:
Generally, midstream stocks follow upstream stocks even though the midstream business is seen as fairly steady. However, the growth rate of midstream companies can be affected by an upstream cyclical downturn because there is less need for additional midstream capacity. DT Midstream (DTM) is priced for years of growth. But in a cyclical industry, a high rate of cyclical growth rarely happens throughout the business cycle. Downturns have a way of bringing about the average rate of growth considerably below the growth rate seen during the better parts of the industry cycle. This is why typical companies become very "cheap" at the top of the business cycle.

Stock Price

The fact that DT Midstream stock is fairly expensive at this point in the business cycle points to an aging bull market. At some point, the threat of losses decreases because a bad year in the stock market has not occurred for a while. Therefore, there is this idea that "keeping up with the competition" is more important for managers in the Wall Street industry, so this becomes more important than safety.

The big thing to consider here is that the price/earnings ratio shown above is very unlikely to expand more. This is yet another midstream company where the market value of the stock is now in the billions. Typically, growth slows as a company gets larger.

Rather than planning on fast growth "going on forever", it is much better to plan for a cyclical downturn that always seems to happen with little notice. It is therefore very likely that growth in the stock price will at best be at whatever rate the market thinks earnings will grow. But a cyclical downturn could decrease the rapid growth that has been the norm to a much slower rate if there is any growth at all. That could mean the loss of more than one year of stock price appreciation when Mr. Market claims to value growth and stock price momentum.

It also likely means that there is a stock price with an asymmetric return that is likely favoring a negative long-term return. At best, the future return would be insufficient to justify the risk of investing in stocks.

Last Article

Since the last article, there was an acquisition completed, and there are growth projects that keep speculators and others interested in the stock even at this price. This is good management, as the results and strategy have demonstrated for quite some time.

However, the price-earnings ratio is now 30 and the dividend yield is far below the typical midstream. As the last article noted, this is very likely a bubble stock. As long as the good news lasts, the bubble is likely to continue (and that could be for some time to come).

Notice that for a company with a price-earnings ratio of 30, the growth rate of EBITDA on a preliminary basis is nothing close to that. Normally you want a growth rate that approximates the price-earnings ratio. In this case the growth rate could end up in the single digits, which does not come close to supporting a price/earnings ratio of 30.

Frankly, the midstream business rarely grows that quickly for long because it services the very cyclical upstream business. The upstream oil and gas business has been contracting cyclically for as long as I can remember, followed by, of course, recoveries and a boom period. This is why growth in this business is known as cyclical growth because growth rarely occurs during a downturn for really any company in the business.

If a midstream company can average a 10% growth rate throughout the business cycle, it will actually be one of the faster-growing midstream companies that I follow. The unfortunate part is that a lot of these fast-growing midstream companies often suffer a mild contraction along with the upstream industry downturn. Clearly a price/earnings ratio of 30 anticipates years of fast growth (without a downturn).

(For any midstream company, not just this one) cyclical downturns usually bring an end to the good news. Growth, if any, is far slower, and a potential small decline is possible. Even though this business is far less volatile than upstream, a slightly negative earnings comparison is anathema to this market and very bad for the current price/earnings ratio.

Whenever I read about or listen to a great investor, they buy when they think they have a bargain, and they sell when their criteria are reached. Rarely do they buy at the exact market bottom. More importantly, they often take profits early, and they really do not worry about the stock price action until that price indicates a need to do something.

On the other hand, many investors comment on my articles about why the stock went up or down 20%. They very much worry about selling too soon.

There is an old saying on Wall Street about "the pigs never win". One of the safety factors about taking profits "too soon" is that you often end up with a profit. That puts an investor far ahead of all those that held to the end (and then some) to turn a profit into a loss.

Investing is all about avoiding big losses. Yet cyclical downturns in stocks often wipe out years of profits (rather than just one). That makes the case for getting out early, like many great investors do.

Maybe the great investors missed out on a stock that transitions into the overvalued range. Then again, investing is about consistent profitability rather than getting "every last cent" out of any one investment idea.

This stock was overpriced for the last article. In fact, it has been overpriced for the last several articles. Generally, my mailbox does not fill up on a stock like this until it is too late. The sad point is those losses were avoidable for as much as a year or two in advance. But judging from the stock price action, I will have a full mailbox at some point in the future.

Summary

I like everything about DT Midstream except the price I have to pay for those characteristics. That price has made this a strong sell for some time. I am a firm contrarian investor. That means I am far more comfortable holding undervalued securities. Something like this does not fit my criteria. I therefore do not go near it.

If this stock is for anyone, it is likely for the technical group. That may well be able to consistently make money using technical analysis. There are stocks out there that are never cheap but can make investors money on a consistent basis.

I write for the buy and hold (and usually forget about it) crowd. This is not the situation for that group of investors.

More importantly, giant price-earnings ratios during the "good times" are often a sign of an aging bull market. That often means the risk of a coming cyclical downturn is heightened because investors are now more concerned with "keeping up with the Joneses" than safety (by worrying about the risk of loss). As a result, investors are often willing to pay a whole lot for given characteristics because they feel that they cannot miss out on this great opportunity. Ironically, this always happens before the floor drops out.

Risks

The good news can keep coming without a cyclical downturn for some time. That could lull an investor "to sleep". Sometimes when losses do not happen for a while, the prices paid would seem to indicate there will not be losses in the future. A knowledgeable investor realizes that when price-earnings ratios are cyclically high, they start planning for what usually happens next even if they do not know when it will happen.

The risk of investment loss increases when someone is willing to pay "anything" for good management and the growth or total return characteristics associated with that management. All good growth stories come to an end sooner or later. Rather than pay a premium for a growth record, it is probably much safer to pay for good growth records without that premium.

The other idea here is that one specific instance may "work". The stock market is all about odds. So, repeating a strategy over time net out to either a loss or an insufficient return. That is far more important than the one time it "works".

If you cannot find investments to invest our money in, then patience is often worth a whole lot of money to wait for those opportunities that do meet your criteria.

With midstream, growth often slows or even abates completely during an upstream cyclical downturn. That alone can result in a big repricing of a stock like this one.

data.title: Capital Southwest: I Went To Dallas For This Safe 11% Dividend Yield Paid Monthly
data.description:
Capital Southwest (CSWC) offers a covered double-digit dividend yield, paid monthly, in an increasingly uncertain space for income investors as a result of Fed rate cuts. The income is the prize from this Dallas-based business development company ("BDC") in an environment where the list of BDCs cutting their dividends continues to grow. Golub Capital (GBDC), CION Investment Corporation (CION), and BlackRock TCP Capital (TCPC) have all recently rightsized their dividends to reflect tighter coverage coming from their floating-rate loans tracking the Fed funds rate lower. This rate currently sits at 3.50% to 3.75% on the back of 75 basis points of rate cuts through 2025 and 100 basis points of cuts through 2024.

CSWC last declared a monthly base cash dividend of $0.1934 per share, unchanged from its prior distribution, and $2.3208 per share annualized for a 10.06% dividend yield. There was also a quarterly supplemental cash dividend of $0.06 per share, for an incremental 1% dividend yield when annualized at $0.24 per share. CSWC's dividend safety is built on three pillars: (1) positive cadence with originations; (2) low non-accrual loan rate; and (3) stock price premium to net asset value ("NAV") on a per share basis.

This aggregate 11% dividend yield is currently safe, provides a 688 basis point spread to the U.S. 10-year Treasury rate (US10Y), and is backed by a balance sheet that most recently received an investment grade rating at "BBB-" by Fitch Ratings. This combination of quality and high yield has attracted significant retail attention towards BDCs, but near-term rate cuts have placed pressure on the spreads these private credit vehicles are able to earn on their respective debt portfolios. CSWC's portfolio was valued at $2.01 billion at fair value and $2.03 billion at cost as of the end of its fiscal 2026 third quarter, ended December 31, 2025. Nonaccruals represented 1.5% of CSWC's investment portfolio at fair value as of the end of the quarter.

I last covered CSWC with a Buy rating in November; since then, some tickers in the BDC industry have seen a sustained selloff on the back of AI-driven fears around software-linked credit. CSWC had 95% of its loans at a floating rate at the end of the third quarter, and expects a roughly $0.06 per share hit to NII per share for every 25 basis points cut to the Fed funds rate. Critically, the CME FedWatch Tool has penciled in 50 basis points of rate cuts through calendar 2026 as its highest probability outcome. This would be somewhat neutral to positive news for BDCs, as it would track lower than the pace of rate cuts seen in 2024 and 2025. This forms the core risk for CSWC, with the BDC's minimal exposure to software companies leaving it broadly spared from the AI-driven selloff seen in February. In my opinion, this selloff is overblown, with my reasons why stated in my coverage of hard-hit Hercules Capital (HTGC).

NAV Premium, Software Credit Fears, And Dividend Safety

CSWC generated third-quarter total investment income of $61.4 million, up 18.23% from its year-ago comp and sequentially from $56.9 million in the second quarter. This growth is being driven by positive momentum with originations, which saw $244 million in total new commitments, $174.7 million of this funded at the end of the quarter. Originations were made to eight new portfolio companies and sixteen existing portfolio companies. This has meant CSWC's investment portfolio realizing both sequential and year-over-year growth to expand its deployed capital base to earn investment income. Net investment income ("NII") came in at $34.6 million, around $0.60 per share, meeting consensus estimates but down by 1 cent from the second quarter and from $0.64 per share a year ago. This dip has come on the back of falling yields in response to Fed rate cuts. CSWC has a lower allocation to software companies, a positioning that has largely spared the common shares from market panic around software-linked debt, with around 90% of the BDC's debt portfolio also being in the more secure first lien credit position of their borrowers debt stack.

The BDC reported a NAV of $16.75 per share, up by 13 cents per share from the second quarter. Crucially, CSWC is currently trading hands at a 36% premium to NAV per share, allowing the BDC the capacity to tap its equity to diversify its capital stack away from just debt. This premium to NAV represents a healthy lever for CSWC to continue to maintain access to institutional capital, allowing for a healthy cadence with originations to continually expand its portfolio even in a credit environment of falling rates. The BDC exited five debt investments and one equity investment for $89.1 million in total proceeds and at a weighted average internal rate of return ("IRR") of 13.1%.

Conclusion

NII per share meant 103.4% coverage for the base cash dividend. Undistributed taxable income earnings spillover came in at $1.02 per share at the end of the quarter. CSWC has escaped market panic around AI and software stocks, and it currently fully covers its monthly dividend distribution on the back of positive momentum with net originations. I am rating CSWC as a buy.

data.title: CareTrust REIT: Normalized FFO Growth Continues Amid Attractive Investment Spreads
data.description:
Introduction

Shares in CareTrust REIT (CTRE) are off to a strong start in 2026, building on the impressive returns delivered in 2025. For a company that uses only 3% debt in its capital structure, the >50% gain in the share price over the last year may clearly cause some investor anxiety and discussions about taking profits.

From a fundamental perspective, CTRE gives few reasons to ditch your shares. Rent collection stands at 100%, and tenant rent coverage is improving year-over-year. As such, I confirm my previous Hold rating on the shares, driven by:

  • A somewhat elevated 20.8x 2026 normalized FFO multiple, partially explained by low leverage.
  • Very attractive normalized FFO growth expectations of 9.4% in 2026.
  • A $500 million investment pipeline, allowing the company to capture the spread between private market yields of about 8.6% and CTRE's market-implied cap rate of 5.7%.

Investors not familiar with CTRE may find the Company Overview section in my previous coverage insightful. The only notable change I would highlight is that enterprise value has now grown by 9% to $9.3 billion, with the latest Q4 2025 results presentation available here.

Q4 2025 Results Overview

CTRE delivered normalized FFO of $0.47/share, up 18% Y/Y. The increase was principally driven by significant investments of $1.5 billion concluded in 2025 at a yield of 8.6%, notably above the company's cost of capital.

Rent collection remained robust at 100%, while EBITDARM coverage (a metric looking at rent coverage by tenants) improved to 2.78x, up 0.11x Y/Y.

CTRE only issued 6.5 million in new shares in Q4 2025, well below the 23 million issued in Q3 2025. This allowed it to deliver $1.76/share in annual normalized FFO, in line with its updated guidance.

All in all, I see no red flags with regard to CTRE's quarterly earnings, with the company delivering sound operating performance. What is more, equity issuance continues at a more moderate pace, allowing CTRE to take advantage of its high market valuation to fund new acquisitions and, at the same time, avoid having to reduce its full-year outlook (which was the case with Q3 2025 results).

Growth continues in 2026

For 2026, the company expects normalized FFO of $1.90-1.95/share, an increase of 9.4% relative to the results achieved in 2025. While the guidance is based on current shares outstanding of 225 million, the company has a $500 million investment pipeline and $372 million in pending proceeds from equity issuance.

As such, depending on the pace of additional investments, I believe CTRE may revise its guidance higher if the investment pipeline is concluded quickly. Alternatively, a slower pace of deploying new equity proceeds may result in a cut to 2026 guidance, as we saw in Q3 2025.

For now, the takeaway for investors is that the company continues to find enough investment opportunities at attractive spreads relative to its cost of capital. Even so, with a growing portfolio, the benefit of incremental investments decreases, as evidenced by the slowing of FFO growth from 18% Y/Y in Q4 2025 to circa 9% in 2026.

Valuation Update

CTRE is currently trading at all-time highs of around $40/share, pointing to a market capitalization of $9 billion and only $0.3 billion in net debt. As such, the REIT's enterprise value has grown to roughly $9.3 billion as of the end of 2025.

I estimate enterprise-level cash flows for 2026 at about $530 million (inclusive of general and administrative expenses of $58 million), or a market-implied cap rate of roughly 5.7%. If we exclude administrative overhead, enterprise-level cash flows amount to roughly $472 million, or a market-implied cap rate of 5.1%.

Either way, we see that the company's market-implied cap rate remains well below the investment yields of 7.7-9.8% achieved over the past decade, indicating that equity issuance at current levels remains highly accretive to normalized FFO.

CTRE Rating

At $40/share, CTRE trades at 20.8x its normalized FFO outlook for 2026, quite elevated for a commercial REIT. The silver lining is that this normalized FFO is achieved with only 3% net debt in the company's capital structure, well below the circa 40% employed by conservative REITs.

With a market-implied 5.1-5.7% and rent escalators of 2.5%, a high single-digit total return remains the base case over the long term. The company can also create value by ongoing investment portfolio growth coupled with stock issuance, although this effect is harder to quantify in the long term since it depends on market conditions (high stock market valuation relative to private market alternatives).

A notable near-term headwind relative to REIT peers is CTRE's limited use of debt. With Fed futures markets pricing in a Fed funds rate around 3% by the end of 2026, more leveraged REIT peers will likely benefit from lower funding costs in both 2026 and 2027.

To conclude, I see limited near-term catalysts for incremental CTRE outperformance relative to REIT peers. At the same time, operating performance remains robust, and the investment pipeline should allow for incremental normalized FFO growth. As such, I confirm my previous Hold rating on the shares.

Risks

As things stand, the risk outlook for CTRE looks quite benign. The company is reporting 100% rent collection, and rent coverage is improving. What is more, the high stock market valuation allows it to grow normalized FFO by issuing shares and investing the proceeds at more attractive private market valuations, something unattainable for other REITs, which trade at a higher market-implied cap rate.

Obviously a change in any of these dynamics may be detrimental for CTRE's share price. For instance, growing investments in senior housing may lead to oversupply in the market, potentially resulting in tenant delinquencies and a weaker rent coverage profile.

Alternatively, a contraction in the spread available between CTRE's public valuation and private market alternatives will diminish the tailwind the company gets from stock issuance. This may principally come from lower private market cap rates, which would be the preferred scenario for shareholders, or a lower CTRE public market valuation, i.e., a significant slump in the share price.

Conclusion

CTRE delivered solid Q4 2025 results, allowing it to reach its revised 2025 full-year outlook. The company expects normalized FFO growth to moderate to about 9.4% in 2026, principally driven by the full-year effect of 2025 investments, 2.5% rent indexation, and incremental capital deployment early in 2026.

The 2026 outlook may be revised higher if CTRE makes swift progress on its $500 million investment pipeline. Alternatively, a slower pace of investments, coupled with ongoing share issuance, may result in a downside revision to the outlook.

I maintain a Hold rating on CTRE as the company has already delivered strong returns so far in 2026, likely indicating that the optimistic normalized FFO growth outlook is priced in. Limited use of debt amid declining interest rates will also put CTRE at a disadvantage relative to more leveraged peers.

CTRE exhibits robust operational performance, indicating limited near-term risks from tenant bankruptcies. Even so, investors should pay close attention to rent collection and rent coverage metrics, which may deteriorate as capital continues to flow into the senior care sector amid attractive returns.

Thank you for reading.

data.title: FUND: Equity Fund With Small And Mid-Cap Exposure, 7.23% Yield And 11.5% Discount
data.description:
Introduction:

Closed-end funds offer an attractive investment class that covers various asset classes and promises high distributions to income investors. They can also offer reasonable total returns if the distributions are reinvested, but generally lag the performance of the S&P 500. They are not for everyone but are particularly appealing to retirees and income investors. However, it will be best to diversify into 7 to 10 CEFs covering many different asset classes and market segments.

During the last decade, the mega- and large-cap stocks have outperformed the small- and mid-cap stocks. Also, the S&P 500 or U.S.-based domestic funds have outperformed almost every other country during the last 10 years, except in 2025. More recently, the foreign stocks have started outperforming the domestic US market. Also, there has been some momentum building in mid-cap and small-cap stocks.

Will this trend continue in 2026 and beyond? It is difficult to predict, but it is likely, primarily due to the valuation gaps. Today's fund, Sprott Focus Trust Fund (FUND), is heavily tilted towards mid-cap and small-cap stocks. In addition, it has nearly 40% of foreign stocks; however, North America accounts for more than 80% of the share. Will this help this fund in the coming year and beyond?

Well, in this backdrop, we think the "FUND" may be worth considering, as it provides significant exposure to non-U.S. markets as well as the small- and mid-cap segment. This fund can provide the necessary diversification to a portfolio that is highly concentrated in large- and mega-cap stocks or funds.

Fund's Background

Sprott Focus Trust (FUND) is a closed-end fund with a long history and trades on the NASDAQ. The fund invests with a long-time horizon and seeks to deliver superior performance to its investors by investing in high-quality businesses with strong balance sheets that may be trading at attractive valuations, in the opinion of the fund manager. The fund's goal is to provide moderate to high income by paying a managed quarterly distribution, which is currently set at 1.50% of trailing NAV (net asset value). However, the fourth-quarter distribution (paid in December) is generally higher than 1.50% of NAV to satisfy the minimum distribution required by the IRS. Also, the fund invests in all sizes of equity stocks, i.e., small-, mid-, and large-cap stocks, but generally favors smaller companies, which makes it different than many funds that are heavily concentrated in large-cap stocks. The fund is well diversified but still focuses and invests in a relatively small number of positions, generally fewer than 50 positions.

As per the fund's literature, its investment Objectives and policies include,

"The Fund's primary investment goal is long-term capital growth. The Fund normally invests at least 65% of its assets in equity securities. The Adviser uses a value approach to invest the Fund's assets in a limited number of domestic and foreign companies. While the Fund is not restricted as to stock market capitalization, the Adviser focuses the Fund's investments primarily in small-cap companies (companies with stock market capitalizations between $500 million and $2.5 billion) and micro-cap companies (companies with stock market capitalizations below $500 million) with significant business activities in the United States. The Fund may invest up to 35% of its assets in direct obligations of the U.S. Government or its agencies and in the non-convertible preferred stocks and debt securities of domestic and foreign companies. The Adviser uses a value method in managing the Fund's assets. In selecting equity securities for the Fund, the Adviser evaluates the quality of a company's balance sheet, the level of its cash flows, and various measures of a company's profitability. The Adviser then uses these factors to assess the company's current worth, basing this assessment on either what it believes a knowledgeable buyer might pay to acquire the entire company or what it thinks the value of the company should be in the stock market. This analysis takes a number of factors into consideration, including the company's future growth prospects and current financial condition. The Adviser invests in the equity securities of companies that are trading significantly below its estimate of the company's "current worth" in an attempt to reduce the risk of overpaying for such companies."

The TTM (12-month trailing) yield of the fund was roughly 7.23%, calculated by taking the average NAV during the year. The current forward yield is estimated to be roughly 6.80% or higher (based on current NAV). The current discount is attractive at -11.5%.

The fund changed its advisers and name from "Royce Focus Trust" to "Sprott Focus Trust" in March 2015.

Other salient features of this fund are as follows:

  • The fund is managed by Sprott Asset Management, Inc.
  • The fund does not use leverage.
  • The fund is an actively managed fund and has a current expense ratio of 1.14%. Also, the fund's expense ratio is capped at 1.20%.
  • Nearly 54% of the assets are invested in companies with less than $5 billion in market capitalization, while the average market capitalization of the portfolio was $4.7 billion (as of Dec. 31, 2025).
  • From 2001 until Jan. 2026, the fund has returned roughly 10.1% on an annualized basis (including distributions), compared to 8.7% from the S&P 500. However, on a 3, 5, 10, and 15-year basis, the performance has been sub-par. However, please note that the fund has higher volatility and drawdowns compared to the S&P 500.
  • As of 12/31/2025, the fund had only 32 equity positions. As such, the fund is well diversified amongst various sectors of the economy. It is invested roughly 60% in the U.S., while the rest is in foreign markets.
  • As of Dec. 31, 2025, the fund had $287.3 million in total (or net) assets.
  • In the year 2025, on the TTM (12-month trailing) basis, the yield was 7.23%, calculated based on the average NAV during the four quarters. The forward yield is estimated to be roughly 6.80% or higher (based on current NAV).

Financial Outlook:

Let's look at the fund's financial health and performance. The most recent report that the fund has released is the semi-annual report for the period from Jan. 1, 2025, to Jun. 30, 2025, and the previous annual report for the period ending Dec. 31, 2024. The annual report for 2025 has not been published yet.

The net investment income (or NII in short) is the net income that a fund earns from its investments in the form of dividends, distributions, interests, and any other generated income (for example, buy-write options) minus all of the fund's expenses, including management fees, operating expenses, commissions, and interest on leverage, if any. For equity-based funds, especially in high-growth sectors like technology, the NII is not very relevant compared to fixed-income funds.

Here is what it looks like in terms of NII, Distributions, and Net Assets at the beginning and end of the statement period.

(All amounts are in US$ (except Shares Outstanding) for the 6-month period ending Jun. 30, 2025, and the 12-month prior period ending Dec. 31, 2024; negative amounts are shown inside parentheses).

Table-1A:

Table-1B: Per Share Data

Distributions:

The fund has a managed distribution policy. It pays a quarterly distribution equal to 1.50% of the trailing NAV (average NAV). This ensures that the shareholders get at least 6% of the trailing NAV on an annual basis. The fact that the fund usually trades at a 10% discount means that the yield on market price is higher than 6%. Moreover, it usually pays a much higher amount during the 4th quarter that exceeds 1.50%. This takes the yield to at least 7% or better.

In the year 2025, on the TTM (12-month trailing) basis, the yield was 7.23%, calculated based on the average NAV during the four quarters. The forward yield is estimated to be roughly 6.80% or higher (based on current NAV and assuming only 1.50% during the 4th quarter).

So, is the distribution covered?

As noted above, FUND follows a managed distribution policy. From the table below, we can see that for the last five and a half years, it has paid all of the distributions from either the investment income or the realized capital gains. However, we noticed in 19(a) that a small amount (less than 2%) of the distribution in the year 2023 was classified as ROC (return of capital). But that is very minor and not a cause for any concern.

We can say that the fund is following a prudent distribution policy and appears very sustainable.

Table-2:

Discount/Premium:

The fund is currently trading at a decent discount of -11.56% (as of 02/09/2026), which is slightly better than its long-term averages. The 3-year and 5-year averages are -10.33% and -9.07%.

However, discount/premium is only one of those factors amongst many that determine the valuation.

Chart-3A: Premium/Discount Information (Past 1 Year)

Chart-3B: Premium/Discount Information (Past 5 Years)

Funds Holdings:

The fund was invested in 32 securities as of Dec. 31, 2025. Even though the fund is diversified amongst many sectors of the economy and the largest position is less than 5% of the assets, the fund is still a bit concentrated, as there are only 32 positions and the top 19 positions account for more than 75% of the assets. Also, there is a high exposure to the materials sector (nearly 40%), while the technology is underrepresented, with less than 3%. The top 10 positions represented nearly 45% of the total assets.

Table-4A: Top 10 Holdings and Sector Allocation (As of Dec. 31, 2025)

Performance:

Here is the performance of the fund, FUND, as presented on the fund's website.

Table-5: Performance as of Dec. 31, 2025

Table-5A: Growth of $10,000 since inception

Let's compare the performance of FUND with similar funds and a blended fund; however, none of them is an exact match. Also included is the S&P 500, though it is quite different than the fund "FUND."

  • (FUND) Sprott Focus Trust Fund
  • (GGZ) Gabelli Global Small & Mid Cap Value Trust)
  • (IWV) iShares Russell 3000 ETF
  • Blended Fund [65% iShares Russell 3000 ETF (IWV), 35% iShares MSCI EAFE Value ETF (EFV)]
  • S&P 500

Table-5A: (As of Dec. 31, 2025)

FUND
